---
title: "1.12 Objects: Instances of Classes"
subtitle: "Part 2: Turtle Class and Dot Operator"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Turtle Class

### Blueprint for turtle objects

![Turtle attributes and methods](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-12-objects-instances-of-classes/turtleOOD.png){fig-align="left" width="28%"}

The `Turtle` class is a blueprint for turtle objects.

It defines attributes for graphical turtles like their color and position and methods to make the turtles move.

Try the Java program below that creates a `Turtle` object called `yertle` using the `Turtle` class.

## Program Code

### `activecode:: TurtleTest`

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.util.*;

public class TurtleTest
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        World habitat = new World(300, 300);
        Turtle yertle = new Turtle(habitat);

        yertle.forward();
        yertle.turnLeft();
        yertle.forward();

        habitat.show(true);
    }
}
```

## Program Check

### `TurtleTest`

Runestone checks that `main()` runs.

The program creates:

::: {.tight-list}
- a `World` object called `habitat`
- a `Turtle` object called `yertle`
:::

Then it asks `yertle` to move forward, turn left, and move forward again.

## Dot Operator

### Ask an object to execute a method

The **dot operator** (`.`) is used to run an object's method.

You can think of the dot as asking the object to do something, or execute one of its methods.

For example:

```java
yertle.forward();
```

asks the turtle `yertle` to go `forward`.

## Dot Operator

### Arguments in parentheses

`yertle.forward()` does not tell `yertle` how much to go forward, so it goes forward `100` pixels by default.

The parentheses `()` after a method name are there in case you need to give the method **arguments** (参数), some data, to do its job.

For example:

```java
yertle.forward(50);
```

asks `yertle` to go forward `50` pixels instead of `100`.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: TurtleTest2`

In the code below, `yertle` goes forward and then turns left.

Change the code to make `yertle` go `forward` twice and then `turnRight`.

## Starter Code

### `TurtleTest2`

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.util.*;

public class TurtleTest2
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        World habitat = new World(300, 300);
        Turtle yertle = new Turtle(habitat);

        // TODO: make yertle go forward twice and then turnRight.
        yertle.forward();
        yertle.turnLeft();

        habitat.show(true);
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### Preserved Runestone checks

Runestone checks:

::: {.tight-list}
- code contains `yertle.forward` two times
- code contains `yertle.turnRight()` at least one time
:::

The goal is to change the movement commands, not the `World` or `Turtle` creation lines.

## Reference Variables

### Object references

A variable like `yertle` of a **reference** type like `Turtle` holds an object reference.

The object reference can be thought of as the memory address of that object.

Variables that are of primitive types like `int` hold just a single number value.

Objects are complex and contain a collection of values called **attributes**, for example the color and size of the turtle.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- identify `Turtle` as a class used to create turtle objects
- explain that `new Turtle(habitat)` creates a `Turtle` object
- use the dot operator to call an object's method
- keep parentheses in method calls
- pass arguments when a method needs data
- distinguish primitive values from reference variables
:::

## End

### 1.12 Part 2 complete

Part 3 continues with creating multiple Turtle objects.
